We were late because the train broke down.
Since two people were sick, we lost a week.
The train broke down, so we were late.
The train broke down. That's why we were late.
Vanessa, why is the launch delayed again?
I understand your frustration. The main cause is that two key people were sick, so we lost almost a week.
A week is a lot. Couldn't someone cover for them?
We tried, but the team struggled to cover the gap. Since we want the product to be solid, we chose to test it properly.
So rushing it could have made things worse?
Exactly. Rushing would lead to bugs. That's why we delayed, to avoid a bad launch.
All right. I appreciate you being clear. When can we launch?
We're aware of the pressure, and we'll solve the last issues by Friday.
The launch is delayed because two key people were sick.
We lost almost a week, so we changed the plan.
Since we want it solid, we chose to test it properly.
Rushing would lead to bugs. That's why we delayed.
We're aware of the pressure, so we'll solve it by Friday.

I want to share something that changed the way people listen to me. For a long time, I just gave answers. We are late. The project failed. I cannot come. Short, flat sentences. And people would nod, but they never really understood me. Then I learned a simple habit. Always say why. Now I do not just say we are late. I say we are late because the supplier missed the deadline. I do not just say the project failed. I say the project failed since we did not have enough time to test it. That tiny word, because, changes everything. It turns a flat statement into a real explanation. And here is the other side of it. I also learned to give the result. We lost a week, so we had to change the plan. The traffic was terrible, that is why I was late. When you connect the cause and the result, the other person can finally follow your thinking. They are not guessing anymore. Honestly, learning to explain my reasons made me sound more professional than any fancy vocabulary ever did. People trust you more when they understand why.

Let me tell you about a difficult conversation I handled well last week. A client was upset because we had to delay the launch. A year ago, I would have just apologized and said nothing useful. This time, I explained. I said, I am sorry for the delay. The reason is that two key people were sick, so we lost almost a week of work. Because we want the product to be solid, we chose to test it properly instead of rushing. I was honest. I did not hide the cause, and I did not blame anyone. I just connected the facts. We had a problem, that is why we needed more time. The client went quiet for a moment, and then he said, thank you for being clear with me. Here is what I learned. People can accept bad news. What they cannot accept is being kept in the dark. When you calmly explain the reason behind a decision, you turn a complaint into a conversation. Since I started doing this, my clients trust me far more, simply because I always tell them why.

First, I state the fact clearly and say we have to delay the launch. Then I give the reason with because and say because two key people were sick. After that, I add the result with so and say so we lost almost a week of work. Next, I connect it all with that is why and say that is why we need a few more days. In the end, I reassure the other person, since we want the product to be solid, we chose to test it properly.
